message:
test(core): property-based tests for auto_tune adaptive control loop (wa-sv4e)

22 proptest properties covering: ParamRange clamping invariants (bounds,
idempotence, monotonicity), AutoTuner range safety under arbitrary metrics
and initial params, gradual change bounds accounting for multi-pressure
compounding, hysteresis prevention of premature adjustments, deadband
no-change guarantee, pinned parameter isolation, direction correctness
for memory/latency/CPU pressures, convergence on constant input, serde
roundtrip with f64 tolerance, and monotonic scrollback decrease under
rising memory pressure.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-5mpu","title":"Design: schema→docs/types strategy (direction, versioning, languages)","description":"# Task: Decide schema-driven generation strategy

## Goal
Choose the practical strategy for schema-driven docs and typed clients.

## Requirements
- Decide generation direction:
  - Rust structs → JSON Schema (preferred for single source of truth)
  - OR JSON Schema → types (and keep schemas authored by hand)
- Decide client target(s):
  - TypeScript (common for tooling)
  - Python (common for scripts)
  - Rust client crate (for in-repo safety)
- Decide versioning policy:
  - how schema versions map to wa versions
  - how breaking changes are detected

## Acceptance Criteria
- Strategy is chosen and consistent with repo goals (robust, low tech debt).

 {"id":"wa-5oq5","title":"Environment detection: detect WezTerm, agents, config state for contextual exercises","description":"# Environment detection for tutorial

## Purpose
Detect the user's environment to provide contextual exercises and skip irrelevant steps. A user without WezTerm running shouldn't do "start watching" exercises.

## Background
Different users have different setups:
- WezTerm running vs not
- Agents present vs empty panes
- wa configured vs fresh install
- Existing DB vs first time

The tutorial should adapt to each situation.

## Implementation Details

### Environment Checks
```rust
pub struct TutorialEnvironment {
    wezterm_running: bool,
    wezterm_version: Option<String>,
    pane_count: usize,
    agent_panes: Vec<AgentInfo>, // Panes with detected agents
    wa_configured: bool,         // wa.toml exists
    db_has_data: bool,           // Segments/events exist
    shell_integration: bool,     // OSC 133 markers detected
}

impl TutorialEnvironment {
    pub async fn detect() -> Self { ... }
    
    pub fn can_run_exercise(&self, exercise: &Exercise) -> CanRun {
        match exercise.requirements {
            Requires::WeztermRunning if !self.wezterm_running => 
                CanRun::No("Start WezTerm first"),
            Requires::AgentPresent if self.agent_panes.is_empty() => 
                CanRun::Simulation("No agents detected, using simulation"),
            _ => CanRun::Yes,
        }
    }
}
```

### Exercise Requirements
Each exercise declares its requirements:
```rust
pub struct Exercise {
    id: ExerciseId,
    title: String,
    requirements: Vec<Requirement>,
    can_simulate: bool,  // True if exercise works in sandbox mode
}
```

### Adaptive Flow
1. User starts track
2. Detect environment
3. For each exercise:
   - Check requirements
   - If met: run exercise
   - If not met but can_simulate: run in sandbox
   - If not met and can't simulate: skip with explanation

## Testing
- Unit tests for each detection check
- Mock tests for different environment states
- Integration tests with real WezTerm (optional, CI skip)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TutorialEnvironment struct with all detection fields
- [ ] detect() function checks all conditions
- [ ] can_run_exercise() returns appropriate guidance
- [ ] Clear user messaging for skipped exercises
- [ ] Tests for all detection paths","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:45:59.975507421Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.203549-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-07T07:22:49.569386524Z"}
+{"id":"wa-5rdx","title":"proptest: ewma invariants","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T20:41:40.313-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T20:41:47.983685-05:00"}

Supports the existing ad-hoc LRU in ingest.rs and general caching needs for pane snapshots, search results, session data.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-12T03:09:27.95307-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T03:15:22.739977-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-12T03:15:22.739977-05:00","close_reason":"LRU cache module: arena-based O(1) operations, 40 tests passing. Committed as 8529ab3d."}
 {"id":"wa-5wge","title":"Secret scan engine (reuse redactor patterns)","description":"## What
Implement a scanner that reuses redaction patterns to detect secrets in stored segments.

## Why
We need a deterministic, safe way to assess exposure without printing secrets.

## How
- Stream segments from storage, run redaction patterns
- Count matches and record redacted snippets (hashes only)

## Success Criteria
- Scanner never emits raw secret values
- Performance is acceptable on large DBs","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:16:20.128394062Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:50.355736-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:50.355744-05:00"}

 {"id":"wa-ssbg","title":"Proactive alerts: threshold monitoring and notification triggers","description":"# Proactive alerts

## Purpose
Alert users before they hit limits or exceed budgets, preventing surprises and enabling proactive optimization.

## Alert Types

### 1. Cost Threshold Alerts
```rust
pub struct CostAlert {
    id: AlertId,
    threshold: Decimal,      // e.g., $50.00
    period: AlertPeriod,     // Day, Week, Month
    current_value: Decimal,
    percent_of_threshold: f32,
}

pub enum AlertLevel {
    Info,      // 50% of threshold
    Warning,   // 75% of threshold
    Critical,  // 90% of threshold
    Exceeded,  // 100%+ of threshold
}
```

### 2. Token Usage Alerts
```rust
pub struct TokenAlert {
    threshold: u64,
    period: AlertPeriod,
    per_agent: Option<AgentType>,
}
```

### 3. Rate Limit Frequency Alerts
```rust
pub struct RateLimitAlert {
    max_hits: u32,     // e.g., 5 per hour
    period: Duration,
}
```

### 4. Account Balance Alerts
```rust
pub struct AccountBalanceAlert {
    account_id: String,
    min_percent_remaining: f32,  // e.g., 15%
}
```

## Alert Configuration
```toml
# wa.toml
[analytics.alerts]
enabled = true

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "cost"
threshold = 50.0
period = "day"
notify = ["terminal"]  # or "webhook", "email"

[[analytics.alerts.rules]]
metric = "account_balance"
threshold = 15  # percent
account = "*"   # all accounts
notify = ["terminal", "webhook"]
```

## Alert Monitoring Service
```rust
pub struct AlertMonitor {
    rules: Vec<AlertRule>,
    storage: StorageHandle,
    notifiers: Vec<Box<dyn Notifier>>,
}

impl AlertMonitor {
    pub async fn check_alerts(&self) -> Vec<TriggeredAlert> {
        let mut triggered = vec![];
        
        for rule in &self.rules {
            let current = self.get_current_value(&rule).await?;
            if let Some(alert) = rule.check(current) {
                triggered.push(alert);
            }
        }
        
        triggered
    }
    
    pub async fn notify_triggered(&self, alerts: &[TriggeredAlert]) -> Result<()> {
        for alert in alerts {
            for notifier in &self.notifiers {
                notifier.notify(alert).await?;
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Notification Channels

### Terminal (CLI output)
```
⚠️  ALERT: Daily cost threshold approaching
    Current: $42.50 / $50.00 (85%)
    Recommendation: Consider pausing non-critical workflows
```

### Status Command Integration
```bash
wa status

# Shows alerts at top
⚠️  1 active alert: Daily cost at 85% of $50 limit

Panes: 3 active...
```

## Testing
- Unit tests for threshold calculations
- Integration tests with mock metrics
- Tests for each notification channel

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cost alerts trigger at configured thresholds
- [ ] Token alerts work per-agent and global
- [ ] Rate limit frequency tracked and alerted
- [ ] Account balance alerts work
- [ ] Terminal notifications clear and visible
- [ ] wa status shows active alerts
- [ ] Tests cover all alert types","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:51:45.285325644Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.331209-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-06T04:37:30.392123379Z"}
 {"id":"wa-ssm4","title":"Auto-Tuning Configuration Parameters","description":"## Goal
Implement automatic tuning of WezTerm/FrankenTerm configuration parameters based on observed system load, replacing static config with adaptive parameters that respond to actual conditions.

## Background & Motivation
WezTerm has dozens of tunable parameters (scrollback size, polling intervals, connection timeouts, etc.) that are currently static. Optimal values depend on runtime conditions:
- 5 panes: aggressive polling, large scrollback → great responsiveness
- 50 panes: same settings → mux server overwhelmed, memory bloated
- High memory pressure: need reduced scrollback, less frequent snapshots
- Idle periods: can afford more expensive operations (garbage collection, snapshots)

Currently users must manually tune these, and static values can't adapt as conditions change throughout the day.

## Technical Design

### Parameter Space
```rust
pub struct TunableParams {
    /// Polling interval for pane state (ms)
    pub poll_interval_ms: u64,          // range: 100-10000
    /// Scrollback lines per pane
    pub scrollback_lines: usize,        // range: 500-10000
    /// Snapshot interval (seconds)
    pub snapshot_interval_secs: u64,    // range: 60-1800
    /// Connection pool size
    pub pool_size: usize,               // range: 1-16
    /// Backpressure threshold
    pub backpressure_threshold: f64,    // range: 0.3-0.9
}
```

### Control Loop
Simple proportional control (not PID — KISS principle):
```rust
pub struct AutoTuner {
    params: TunableParams,
    targets: TuningTargets,
    history: VecDeque<SystemMetrics>,
}

pub struct TuningTargets {
    /// Target RSS as fraction of available memory
    pub target_rss_fraction: f64,       // default: 0.5
    /// Target mux response latency (ms)
    pub target_latency_ms: f64,         // default: 10.0
    /// Target CPU utilization fraction
    pub target_cpu_fraction: f64,       // default: 0.3
}

impl AutoTuner {
    pub fn tick(&mut self, metrics: &SystemMetrics) -> TunableParams {
        self.history.push_back(metrics.clone());

        // Memory pressure → reduce scrollback, increase snapshot interval
        if metrics.rss_fraction > self.targets.target_rss_fraction {
            let pressure = metrics.rss_fraction / self.targets.target_rss_fraction;
            self.params.scrollback_lines = (self.params.scrollback_lines as f64 / pressure) as usize;
            self.params.snapshot_interval_secs = (self.params.snapshot_interval_secs as f64 * pressure) as u64;
        }

        // Latency pressure → reduce polling, increase pool
        if metrics.mux_latency_ms > self.targets.target_latency_ms {
            let pressure = metrics.mux_latency_ms / self.targets.target_latency_ms;
            self.params.poll_interval_ms = (self.params.poll_interval_ms as f64 * pressure) as u64;
        }

        self.params.clamp_to_ranges();
        self.params.clone()
    }
}
```

### Safety Rails
- All parameters have hard min/max ranges (never go below safety floor)
- Changes are gradual (max 10% change per tick)
- Hysteresis: don't oscillate — require sustained signal before changing
- Manual override: user can pin any parameter, auto-tuner skips it
- Telemetry: log every adjustment with reason

### Galaxy-Brain Card
```
+- Auto-Tuner ----------------------------------------------------------------+
| Panes: 47  |  RSS: 12.3GB (62% of 20GB target)                              |
|                                                                               |
| Adjusted Parameters:                                                          |
|   poll_interval:    500ms -> 750ms  (latency pressure: 1.5x)                |
|   scrollback_lines: 3500 -> 2800   (memory pressure: 1.24x)                 |
|   snapshot_interval: 300s -> 300s   (unchanged)                              |
|                                                                               |
| System adapting to 47-pane load. Reduced polling frequency                    |
| to lower mux server pressure.                                                |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### Implementation Location
- New: crates/wa-core/src/auto_tune.rs
- Integration: main event loop reads tuned params each tick
- Config: tuning targets and parameter ranges

## Existing Code References
- backpressure.rs: has hardcoded thresholds (0.50/0.75) — auto-tuner can adjust these
- watchdog.rs: fixed timeout thresholds — auto-tuner adapts to load
- tailer.rs: adaptive polling with 1.5x backoff — auto-tuner sets base rate

## Configuration
```toml
[auto_tune]
enabled = true
tick_interval_secs = 30
target_rss_fraction = 0.5
target_latency_ms = 10.0
target_cpu_fraction = 0.3
max_change_per_tick = 0.1    # Max 10% change per tick
hysteresis_ticks = 3         # Sustained signal for 3 ticks before changing
```

## Dependencies
- Telemetry pipeline (wa-3kxe.5): provides system metrics (RSS, latency, CPU)
- Enhances continuous backpressure (wa-1qz1.6): auto-tuner can adjust center threshold
- Enhances adaptive watchdog (wa-1qz1.7): auto-tuner adapts sensitivity_k based on load

## Acceptance Criteria
- Proportional control loop adjusting 5+ parameters
- Hard safety ranges (never exceed bounds)
- Hysteresis to prevent oscillation
- Manual override per parameter
- Galaxy-brain transparency card
- Telemetry logging for every adjustment
- Unit tests: synthetic load curves, verify parameter adaptation
- Integration test: simulated memory pressure, verify scrollback reduction

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/auto_tuning.rs`:
  - `parameter_search_latency`: measure time for one full tick() cycle (read metrics, compute adjustments, clamp ranges). Target: <100us per tick with 5 tunable parameters and 100-entry history.
  - `hysteresis_evaluation`: measure time to evaluate whether sustained signal threshold is met across hysteresis_ticks window. Target: <10us per parameter.
  - `clamp_to_ranges`: measure range clamping for all parameters. Target: <1us (pure arithmetic).

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Tuning convergence**: for any constant SystemMetrics input repeated for 100+ ticks, parameters converge to stable values (change per tick approaches zero). The control loop does not oscillate indefinitely.
- **Range invariant**: for any sequence of SystemMetrics inputs (including extreme values like 0.0, 1.0, f64::MAX), all output parameters remain within their configured [min, max] ranges after clamp_to_ranges().
- **Monotonic response**: if `metrics.rss_fraction` increases monotonically over consecutive ticks, `scrollback_lines` decreases monotonically (or stays at minimum). Higher pressure always results in equal or more aggressive reduction.
- **Manual override respected**: if a parameter is pinned by the user, the auto-tuner never modifies it regardless of input metrics. `tuned_params.pinned_field == original_value` for all tick sequences.

## Cross-References
- **wa-1qz1.7** (Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter): the Kalman filter in wa-1qz1.7 provides filtered, noise-reduced metric signals that the auto-tuner can consume instead of raw metrics. This prevents the auto-tuner from reacting to transient spikes. The auto-tuner's hysteresis and the Kalman filter's smoothing are complementary noise-rejection mechanisms.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:15.747809Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-12T01:42:27.487228-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-12T01:42:27.487228-05:00","close_reason":"Auto-tuning module fully implemented: proportional control loop (5 params), hard safety ranges, hysteresis, manual overrides (PinnedParams), adjustment logging, 23 passing tests (19 unit + 4 proptest), Criterion benchmarks added (benches/auto_tuning.rs). Fixed bugs: calm_metrics deadband values, convergence proptest threshold/ticks for competing-pressure drift.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:33.154694Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.7","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:56.373305Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-ssm4","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:52.939354Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

All tests pass."}
 {"id":"wa-sx55","title":"Implement wa-side native event listener for vendored WezTerm events","description":"## Overview

Implement the wa-side Unix socket listener that receives events from vendored WezTerm's native integration. This replaces the Lua→CLI→IPC path with direct socket communication.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-3gon (Protocol design)
- Completed: wa-20fw (WezTerm fork emitting events)

## Implementation

### 1. Event Listener Module

Create \`crates/wa-core/src/native_events.rs\`:

\`\`\`rust
use std::path::PathBuf;
use tokio::net::{UnixListener, UnixStream};
use tokio::sync::broadcast;

/// Events received from native WezTerm integration.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum NativeEvent {
    PaneOutput {
        pane_id: u64,
        data: Vec<u8>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    StateChange {
        pane_id: u64,
        title: String,
        rows: u16,
        cols: u16,
        is_alt_screen: bool,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    UserVarChanged {
        pane_id: u64,
        name: String,
        value: String,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneCreated {
        pane_id: u64,
        domain: String,
        cwd: Option<String>,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
    PaneDestroyed {
        pane_id: u64,
        timestamp: u64,
    },
}

/// Listener for native WezTerm events.
pub struct NativeEventListener {
    socket_path: PathBuf,
    listener: UnixListener,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
}

impl NativeEventListener {
    /// Create and bind the event listener socket.
    pub async fn bind(socket_path: PathBuf) -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Remove stale socket if exists
        let _ = std::fs::remove_file(&socket_path);
        
        // Create parent directory
        if let Some(parent) = socket_path.parent() {
            std::fs::create_dir_all(parent)?;
        }
        
        let listener = UnixListener::bind(&socket_path)?;
        let (event_tx, _) = broadcast::channel(1024);
        
        Ok(Self {
            socket_path,
            listener,
            event_tx,
        })
    }
    
    /// Subscribe to events.
    pub fn subscribe(&self) -> broadcast::Receiver<NativeEvent> {
        self.event_tx.subscribe()
    }
    
    /// Run the event listener loop.
    pub async fn run(&self) -> Result<(), Error> {
        loop {
            let (stream, _) = self.listener.accept().await?;
            let event_tx = self.event_tx.clone();
            
            tokio::spawn(async move {
                if let Err(e) = handle_connection(stream, event_tx).await {
                    tracing::warn!("Native event connection error: {}", e);
                }
            });
        }
    }
}

async fn handle_connection(
    stream: UnixStream,
    event_tx: broadcast::Sender<NativeEvent>,
) -> Result<(), Error> {
    use tokio::io::{AsyncBufReadExt, BufReader};
    
    let reader = BufReader::new(stream);
    let mut lines = reader.lines();
    
    while let Some(line) = lines.next_line().await? {
        match parse_event(&line) {
            Ok(event) => {
                let _ = event_tx.send(event);  // Ignore if no subscribers
            }
            Err(e) => {
                tracing::debug!("Failed to parse native event: {}", e);
            }
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}

fn parse_event(json: &str) -> Result<NativeEvent, Error> {
    let value: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_str(json)?;
    
    match value.get("type").and_then(|t| t.as_str()) {
        Some("pane_output") => Ok(NativeEvent::PaneOutput {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            data: base64::decode(value["data"].as_str().unwrap_or(""))?,
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        Some("state_change") => Ok(NativeEvent::StateChange {
            pane_id: value["pane_id"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
            title: value["state"]["title"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string(),
            rows: value["state"]["rows"].as_u64().unwrap_or(24) as u16,
            cols: value["state"]["cols"].as_u64().unwrap_or(80) as u16,
            is_alt_screen: value["state"]["is_alt_screen"].as_bool().unwrap_or(false),
            timestamp: value["ts"].as_u64().unwrap_or(0),
        }),
        // ... other event types
        _ => Err(Error::UnknownEventType),
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Integration with Watcher

Modify \`crates/wa-core/src/watcher.rs\` or equivalent:

\`\`\`rust
impl Watcher {
    pub async fn run_with_native_events(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        let native_listener = NativeEventListener::bind(
            self.config.native_event_socket.clone()
        ).await?;
        
        let mut native_rx = native_listener.subscribe();
        
        // Spawn listener task
        let listener_handle = tokio::spawn(async move {
            native_listener.run().await
        });
        
        loop {
            tokio::select! {
                // Existing poll loop
                _ = self.poll_interval.tick() => {
                    self.poll_panes().await?;
                }
                
                // Native events
                Ok(event) = native_rx.recv() => {
                    self.handle_native_event(event).await?;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    
    async fn handle_native_event(&mut self, event: NativeEvent) -> Result<()> {
        match event {
            NativeEvent::PaneOutput { pane_id, data, .. } => {
                // Feed to pattern detection
                self.pattern_engine.process(&data);
                // Feed to screen state tracker
                self.screen_tracker.process_output(pane_id, &data);
            }
            NativeEvent::StateChange { pane_id, is_alt_screen, .. } => {
                // Update pane state directly (no polling needed)
                self.pane_states.update_alt_screen(pane_id, is_alt_screen);
            }
            NativeEvent::UserVarChanged { pane_id, name, value, .. } => {
                // Same handling as IPC user-var events
                self.handle_user_var(pane_id, &name, &value).await?;
            }
            // ... other events
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
\`\`\`

### 3. Feature Flag

Gate native event support behind a feature:

\`\`\`toml
# crates/wa-core/Cargo.toml
[features]
native-wezterm = []
\`\`\`

\`\`\`rust
#[cfg(feature = "native-wezterm")]
mod native_events;
\`\`\`

### 4. Configuration

Add config option:

\`\`\`toml
# wa.toml
[native]
enabled = true
socket_path = "/tmp/wa/events.sock"
\`\`\`

## Performance Comparison

| Metric | Lua Path | Native Path |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| Event latency | ~50-100ms | ~1-5ms |
| Process spawns | 1 per event | 0 |
| CPU overhead | High (Lua) | Low (Rust) |
| Memory overhead | Lua VM | Minimal |

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] NativeEventListener implemented
- [ ] All event types parsed correctly
- [ ] Integration with watcher loop
- [ ] Feature-gated code
- [ ] Configuration support
- [ ] Unit tests for event parsing
- [ ] Integration test with mock events
- [ ] Benchmark: latency comparison

## Dependencies

- Depends on: wa-3gon (protocol design)
- Depends on: wa-20fw (WezTerm fork emitting events)

## Files to Create/Modify

- NEW: crates/wa-core/src/native_events.rs
- crates/wa-core/src/lib.rs — add mod native_events
- crates/wa-core/src/watcher.rs — integration
- crates/wa-core/src/config.rs — native config options
- crates/wa-core/Cargo.toml — feature flag

 {"id":"wa-t63d","title":"E2E script: secret redaction in audit/export (no raw secrets in artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E script — secret redaction in audit/export

## Goal
Prove that end-to-end audit and export paths never leak secrets from action inputs.

This is a trust-critical property:
- users will paste artifacts into issues
- CI will upload artifacts
- we must ensure secrets are not present in these outputs

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.
- Ensure policy allows a send in a safe context.

Steps
1) Send a string containing a known fake secret pattern (e.g., `sk-FAKESECRET123`) via `wa send` or `wa robot send`.
2) Export audit trail slice for this run (or query audit feed).
3) Assert that:
  - the audit entry exists
  - the raw secret string does NOT appear anywhere in audit export output
  - a redacted placeholder DOES appear (to prove redaction happened, not omission)

## Assertions
- Audit emission records the action.
- Redaction rules remove the secret from:
  - audit DB row(s)
  - audit export output
  - E2E script logs + artifacts

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Audit export JSONL.
- Script stdout/stderr.
- Grep-style proof output (counts of secret matches should be 0).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic.
- Any failure clearly indicates whether the leak came from:
  - audit emission
  - redaction engine
  - export path
  - e2e script logging


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - The script must scan *all* produced artifacts (logs, JSONL exports, summaries) for the fake secret string.
  - Include at least two different fake secret patterns (OpenAI-style, GitHub token-style) to ensure redactor coverage isn’t overly narrow.

- Failure mode:
  - If a match is found, print the exact artifact filename + surrounding context (bounded) to make the leak diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-t806","title":"Implement `wa approve --dry-run`: preview approval token validation and grant","description":"
# wa approve --dry-run Implementation

## Purpose
Let users verify that an approval code is valid and see what action it would permit before actually granting approval.

## Output Format
```
DRY RUN - No changes will be made

Approval Token Validation:
  Token: abc123
  Status: ✓ Valid
  Created: 2026-01-18T14:30:00Z
  Expires: 2026-01-18T15:30:00Z

Associated Action:
  Type: SendText
  Pane: 9 (codex @ /project)
  Content: "sudo rm -rf /tmp/cache"
  Reason: RequireApproval (destructive pattern match)

Would grant:
  - Allow-once permission for this specific action
  - Action will execute immediately after approval
  - Audit log entry will be created

To approve for real:
  wa approve abc123
```

## Implementation
1. Parse approval token
2. Look up pending approval request
3. Validate token hasn't expired
4. Display associated action details
5. Don't actually modify approval state

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Shows approval token validation status
- [ ] Displays the action that would be permitted
- [ ] Shows expiration warning if near expiry
- [ ] JSON output for robot mode

## Testing
- Unit tests for dry_run flag propagation and no-side-effect guards.
- Integration tests: verify no DB/lock/pane mutation under dry-run.
- E2E: add a dry-run scenario with verbose logs and artifacts.
